title: Emily Gutzmer -Hiring Manager Screen | Client Operations Manager - Agency
meeting_title: Emily Gutzmer -Hiring Manager Screen | Client Operations Manager - Agency

transcript.text: This meeting is being recorded.
Hi, Emily.
There we go.
Hi, how are you?
Good, how are you?
Doing well.
Where are you calling from?
Austin, Texas.
I am in the Northeast.
I'm in Rhode Island.
Oh, okay.
But we have a couple of Texans on the team now.
think it's three, maybe four.
Okay.
Yeah.
Austin area or sort of spread out through?
One person's in Houston and the other two are in Austin.
That makes sense.
Yeah, probably about on par with what you'd expect.
Yeah, yeah.
There's so much.
I don't know, the past like 10 years, Austin's grown so much that I would be surprised if there wasn't someone.
I'm sure, yeah.
Cool.
Well, I can kick us off by giving you a little bit of background on myself, how I'm thinking about the role, growthx, and because I am the hiring manager, so this person will be reporting to me.
And then we can talk about you, and then I'll leave some time at the end for you to ask questions.
These usually aren't 45 minutes, so, you know, we can take as much or as little of the time as we need.
But, yeah.
So, I'm Andi.
I lead customer operations for GrowthX, which really means right now I'm owning our delivery org.
So, GrowthX is...

So essentially today, services model, we're operating as a marketing agency, but we have our own platform that we're building internally that will, it's what our team leverages to, like, put out and develop content using AI workflows.
Eventually, that platform will become external facing.
So we will be kind of a more traditional SaaS company with a services model on top.

But the beauty of what we're doing now means that our services model can sort of help pay for our product development and also act as, like, a feedback loop because we're leveraging the platform, like, our team is using it to develop content.

So I'm thinking a lot about right now, just, like, how to, like, team efficiency, customer satisfaction, like, how to measure health, make sure we're, we're, this is our first, we just came upon one year of.
Being in existence.
So renewals, how we think about those going forward.
What do expansions look like right now?
We kind of have like one main work stream we're selling.
So like, what does kind of that conversation look like?
And also, how do we get better at forecasting, tracking sort of throughout a client lifecycle, health, measure ICP, things like that.

And then, you know, with all of those things in mind, also, how are we forecasting like team efficiency and where our team, like how to leverage our team across the different projects and clients that we're serving.
So like right now, we're actually, I'm actually working on a reorg on the back end because things change a lot.
So what I am hoping for in someone in kind of like the ops manager is someone who can help me implement and who has implemented.
We project management and tracking tools right now.
We have a team of directors who are leveraging linear for strategic projects for clients.
We also use Notion for tracking health scoring and ongoing updates.
We have HubSpot, but we don't have a lot of seats in HubSpot.
So we're tracking a bunch of things in a lot of different places.
As we think about kind of what's happening moving forward, I need somebody who can help me kind of implement the changes that we want to make, work on the systems and building out the tooling.
But I think one of the exciting things about this role is the fact that we are, have this like product coming out.
We're moving so quickly.
We're growing so quickly that like, you know, I had somebody the other day ask about what does like the career pathing look like for this role.
And I don't have an answer because I think there are a lot of different directions you could take this.
Like, we don't have support today because it's...
It's all services-based, so you just talk to your account manager, but if we turn it into a product, like, you know, there's a million directions that somebody with this skill set could go in.
So that's how I'm thinking about this role and how I'm selling this role also.
There's a ton of work to be done because, as I said, we're working in a lot of different systems.
So looking for someone who can help me, like, kind of pull, analyze, track all of the pieces that we're slowly starting to assemble right now.
Gotcha.
Okay.
That all makes sense.
That sounds very familiar.
I'm coming out of an early-stage startup, and so we used Linear, we used Notion, we used HubSpot.
There weren't many seats.
Like, all of that sounds very familiar.
Yeah, so on my end, I have been in tech for a while.
I've been on the customer-facing side for a significant chunk of that.
I was a customer success manager at a few different places.
At Airtable, I came in as a customer success manager, but it's, like, like...
Oh,
Essentially a data, like a database and also workflow management tool.
so I found that in being a customer success manager, it was sort of like operational consultative work.
And those were the conversations, you know, they were just more interesting conversations than other CSM kind of conversation types I've been having.
And so that really started to like, I don't know, I guess tickle the back of my brain that maybe ops was like something that I would be a little bit more interested in.
so that Airtable is where I made that move over into the strategy and operations team.
And I took a role to own a platform that the customer success managers were using.
So it was still very closely tied to like the team that I had been on.
Honestly, it was a platform that had rolled out and it was not rolled out well.
And I was a very loud detractor.
at some point someone was like, maybe you should just take that job to fix it.
So that was sort of the jump over.
And then at my last company, it was a very early stage startup.
And I was, we just didn't do.
was well in our Series A funding.
So I was laid off like right as we were finishing up Series A, but like very, very early stage and had a lot of the similar kind of similar things.
We had no health tracking.
We had no sense of like what data do we need to be collecting to start to build up some of this infrastructure.
And there was just this like look ahead vision of like, how do we even scale if we don't have our foundation set?

And so that was really my prerogative coming in to that customer operations role was like getting those foundation stones set so that we could grow in a way that kept us, agile, you could kind of build for a couple of different paths as you went, but making sure we were collecting the same data from all of our clients, getting some of those early reporting structures in place, getting some customer health dashboarding in place.
We launched Asana as a project management tool, but our tech teams were using linear.

figuring out like where those boundaries were and when you went to which tool and just, you know, figuring out some of those internal workflows, which a lot going on, but it was all like very interesting and a great team.
Like everyone was great to work with.
So I really enjoyed that piece of it.
enjoyed of it.
Awesome.
My last company was a Series A that didn't raise its Series B, so understood on the kind of startup life.
Yeah, that's the way it goes.
Cool.

Well, you talked about taking over your, like, the op system at Airtable, and maybe you have another example like this, but could you walk me through a workflow that you built, like, end-to-end that improved, like, delivery or customer success?
Yeah.
At Curated4U, I launched Zendesk.
So when I came in, we had no ticketing platform and no real, I guess, consistent way for clients to, like, send in requests or provide feedback on the deliverables we were sending them.
And so what we did, sort of, like, someone higher up, I think the COO came to me and just said, we need a ticketing system to solve this problem of, like, how do we grow if we can't even?
And so when I was thinking about what does this actually look like in practicality, it also meant like changing how we supported our clients.

They, you know, essentially asking our clients to talk directly to the services team, the merchandising team, and bypass our customer success managers because they didn't need to be that middle person.
But we'd had a very like hands-on feel that was a big internal change to make.
And so there was this both designing the workflow, but then also the sense of like internal change management and the enablement that went into like expanding an account team, putting more people.
Yeah.
Like dedicated people on accounts.
Yeah.
Yeah.
And this was luckily very easy.
So like in terms of setting up automations, it was a pretty clear platform.
So I had routing logic.
So that by, you know, account by account basis, those tickets would get sent to the right person on the merchandising team.

We were starting to track points around like how quickly we could resolve those tickets, understanding what the current status looked like, trying to understand like what kinds of feedback we were getting in just bucketing, bucketing tickets so that we could see, you know, is this something that fundamentally we need to change our AI system on the back end to be better about?
Is this something that's more about time to value?
Just understanding where the pain points were when we got those in.
And then did the client enablement.
So hopped on calls with clients and ran them through.
Here's how it's going to work from this point on modeled opening a ticket, gave them, you know, drafted and gave them resources.
I also did the internal enablement with the new team members, everyone who's going to be working in the platform.
There were a couple of different roles.
So people who were coming in to like be a new account team member.
We also did a lot about.
How do you talk to clients?
There's a lot of like fresh out of college people who hadn't like had a lot of that experience.
So we sort of ramped up some of that like, you know, professional environment experience.
And then, you know, internally had like consistent alignment conversations with leadership.
We, I had a roadmap with our tech team about how does this eventually integrate into our own app so that our clients didn't feel like they had multiple places that they had to go.
was a unified experience.
So I had a roadmap built out for that, just making sure that everything we did was in alignment with what other teams were focused on.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Awesome.
What was like the biggest resistance or challenge in that, in that running that project?
I think that's a good question.
I think there were like two, not huge, but kind of equally consistent pain points.
One was from a top down perspective.
One
We'd get this, like, mismatch in urgency, right?
So, like, one week, everyone's in alignment.
We know exactly what the time frame should look like.
We know exactly what is going to happen on this platform this week.
And then next week, it'd be like, no, we need to launch it, like, tomorrow.
We have this client coming on, and they need to launch with it, you know?
And so, like, just constantly kind of managing, no, we've already aligned on this.
Is this something we need to change?
If we change it, what's the cost of that?
And keeping those conversations, like, open and transparent.
And then the other end was just, you know, it was a huge shift in how the teams work together and where data could be found.
And that was something where we hit, like, pretty easily, maybe 70% success very early.
But upping that was kind of hard.
People, our team members were still going to, like, their old resources or their offline notes and, like, keeping things over there.
So just trying to centralize some of these data points that we wanted to, like, get better reporting or have, you know, client preferences in, like, their market.
Merchandising rules and their expectations for deliverables in a centralized place.
Some of that piece was a little bit harder to get, like, closer to 100%, even 90, 95% would have been a great, great spot to hit.
And that took a little bit more.
So we ramped up halfway pretty easily, but getting that final half was tougher.
Yeah.
Yeah, I can imagine.
Stop putting this in your own workspace.
Right.
Please send that in every job I've ever had.
Yes.
Yeah, exactly.
Cool.
So can you talk me through, I would expect this was especially true at your last role, but, like, you come in, you have a ton of operational problems to solve.
How do you think about, like, prioritizing across all of the projects that you might face and deciding what to solve first?
about, about, what
Yeah.
The first thing I always want to know or understand better is like what the company level goal is and make sure that a lot of, at least a good chunk of the work is supporting whatever those OKRs are.
That was something when I came into Curated for You, we didn't even have very clear OKRs.
So was a lot more like there's a lot more freedom in prioritizing.

But, you know, I think in growing organizations, as some of that stuff gets more centralized, just making sure that like at least a good chunk of the work is directly supporting one of those and understanding priority from there.
But even within that, I am often thinking about urgency and like how real that urgency is.
For the last example about like we have to launch tomorrow, we probably didn't have to launch tomorrow.
So understanding what that urgency really looks like, understanding blockers, like what what work is going to unlock other pieces of work that we wanted.
be able to work on down the line.

And then potential impact, like something that is going to change things for 100 team members is probably going to be higher priority for me than something that impacts like five, unless that five is very low hanging fruit.
So I guess then the lift is the fourth piece is understanding like what goes into it.

Those smaller impact pieces are fine if it's also not very much work, but I'm not going to likely prioritize a really big robust project that changes things for three people unless there's like something else feeding into that prioritization.
Yeah.
Awesome.
Okay.
And then, can you tell me about a time that you worked through conflicting priorities between teams to complete a deliverable?
Yeah.
Yeah.
Man, I feel like that happens all the time.
One of the things that I did at Curated for You was revamp our onboarding process for our clients.
And so I would say the deliverable, like the deliverable in and of itself was this was a process.
It was here's how we onboard our clients.
Here's the asset that we work out of.
Here's how we communicate it with our clients.

But that was something that was very customized, like coming in, we had a different onboarding flow for every client, depending on what their platform was, how our client worked, who we were working with at our client.
And so at any point, you could ask anyone at the company across any team, like, how do we onboard clients and get a different answer.

And so, you know, our customer success team and our sales team, which are really one in the same, very much prioritized, like a smooth customer experience, looking very buttoned up to our clients, like having this very pulled together.
All of our information's in the same place.
We know the answers to the questions that you've already talked to us about kind of feel.
And making sure that we could deliver value pretty quickly.
And our tech team was much more concerned with how does this fit into the rest of our work?
How much work is this going to be?
We can't do custom applications for everybody.
They were much more concerned with the bandwidth and the cost of the bandwidth that would be taken up on other pieces of the roadmap that they were working on on the side.
And so basically we were at a point where like the head of tech and the head of CS just argued every time they talked about it.
So like no progress could be made because it was just an argument and no one wanted to be in the room with them.
So I got pulled in.
But what I did was just talk to them each, you know, I was in some of the onboarding conversations.
I got a sense of like, here's how the conversations are going right now.
Worked with everyone involved individually, just like grabbed 30 minutes.
Like tell me how it works from your perspective.
Tell me me how how how it it works from
The pain points.
Tell me the things you're frustrated about.
I'm not recording this call.
won't get out.
Like, I want to know what is making your life hard about this and also what you want out of it, what it would look like if it was going well, right?
And then I really started with a framework.
I wanted it to be something that could be flexible, feel like there's a structure that we can be flexible within, if that makes sense.
So, you know, understanding here's how much time we're taking out or planning for from the technical integration perspective, what that looks like might be different based on platform.
So understanding what those decision trees could be and building in room for that and flexibility for that.
But I just simplified everything.
Like, we want to make sure we get our paperwork in line.
We want to make sure we're talking to the merchandising team and we're answering these five core questions.
We want to make sure our tech team is pulled in now and we already have this information for them.
So they're set up for success.
We want the tech team to put their findings in this document so we all can find it afterwards.
And just like really giving those bare bones structures to keep things centralized.
And as that, you know, we used it, it worked well.

And then as that kept going, we started to add in some platform specific things, you know, especially moving into Asana where you could automate a little bit, it was a lot easier to say, they're on Shopify and auto populate five core tasks that wouldn't have existed if it was Salesforce or another platform.
So that was where there was like a little bit of room for future growth and building it out to be more robust in a responsive way.
Yeah.
Awesome.
Um, what's the like project that you led that you're most proud of?
Oh, another good question.
I feel like it's the ticketing and then the customer health score.
Um, for us to get a customer health score ramped up, it went all the way back to going to our clients and asking for the data differently.
Um, like we were getting our reporting in, in very customized ways.
We were getting a lot of exports from their back.
Back-end platforms that all looked different, all had different numbers, all had numbers defined slightly differently.
And so it was one of these things that was kind of always going on in the background of trying to improve and get to a good place in data integrity and hiding.
But what that enabled for us then was like customer-specific reporting, where it was much more apples to apples.
We could see like the same numbers across all of our clients.

And then that kind of unlocked customer health scoring, where we could pull the data that we were getting in from them, supplement it with data from other teams, and get like an actual customer health score that we were then able to test a little bit.

And so, you know, it was honestly probably like three or four small projects, but in terms of just having a roadmap and, you know, over the course of maybe eight months, continuously making progress towards it through these smaller projects, I think that last dashboard was like this beautiful, shiny color-coded thing that I loved.
Did you build the dashboard?
Yeah, I did.
That's great.
What were the, like, points that you guys used as your, like, what were the sort of components of a health score for your, for you?
Yeah, I had a, there was a relationship strength pillar.
So it was like a, it was a pillar setup.
There were three or four pillars, and then those would all get weighted and rolled up into it.
that's what we have.
Yeah.
perfect.
So yeah, relationship health was one.
And so that was, you know, champion development, it was the temperature of the client, are they mad at us?
Do they engage with us?
Do they ignore us in our weekly calls?
Or do they actually talk to it?
You know, all of those pieces, there was a channel expansion score.
So understanding and reporting on how kind of like multifaceted we, we could get in supporting them, or are we in their social channels, their email channels, etc.
And there was a KPIs pillar.
And that's where like revenue, like the e-commerce metrics came in.
Are we really?
Driving performance for them.
The piece that I was working on to supplement that was also, we call them curations, but like a deliverable score.
So understanding for the contracted deliverables, how are we stacking up against that?
we tracking to like fulfill the full contract?
Understanding time to value, how long is it taking to ramp one up and get that out to our client?
And understanding the feedback on them, which is where the ticketing piece would come in.
And, you know, are they giving us really big?
Our vibe is off feedback.
Are we getting really small nitpicky feedback?
Are they trusting us and just not giving any feedback because it's working fine?
And just trying to get that like client temperature from that perspective.
But that was in a draft mode by the time I left.
So that had not been fully implemented.
Yeah.
Awesome.
I've said awesome a lot, but it seems to a word of choice.
Anyways, those were all the questions I had.
What questions do you have for me?
Cool.
I had a couple.
I'd be curious.
So I guess there's like some very clear, like by six months, we would want you to do X, Y, Z in the job description.
But I'm curious, backing up a step, like what the vision or the goals are for the next year that this role would be kind of supporting the work for?
Yeah, so with the idea in mind that we will be launching like a product side to the business or like a PLG side to the business.
I think I would want someone who can help me think through like the full design of a delivery org, like a client facing org that serves both like the product and that funnels into services.
Like we have some very basic models on that, but like, I just think that the evolution.
of what the interaction between product and services looks like is going to be a huge piece of focus for our business unit for the next year.
And the way that specifically services integrates, we're launching, we are also launching a subscription-based community.

So I think somebody who can help think through and model what the client ecosystem looks like because it's going to be really hairy to map all of that and think through health tracking and opportunity scoring.
I'm sure if we get to that point, it will not just be you and I.
There's a much bigger team.
I think we're already thinking about hiring a data analyst.
Specifically, we don't have one of those.
But like-
Andi there's a lot of complexity in the, just like the structure of how we make the business like sustainable, efficient across all of these offerings.
And so I think that, I don't know like what the specific projects or pieces of that look like.
Like, I know the first thing that I want to do is have like a project platform, probably similar to what you implemented in Airtable.
Hopefully not that same platform that you hated, but.
It's a different, it's a different.

But like, you know, tracking, like bringing everything together and then figuring out, okay, how do we make sure that even as we're adding on these different pieces of the business, defining additional work streams and the services side, the product piece, like how do we make sure that we still have a unified place where all of that is visible?
We can track the work, we can track the people, we can like, you know.
Come up with, and again, that would not just be like this person's job, but like, that's what's happening in the next year.
And those are like, that's the top level problem that I know this person will, will like be very useful in working on.
Yeah.
Okay.
That makes sense.
And I imagine some of my next question is answered in that, but I'm curious, like, what are the big pain points that you would imagine this role kind of hopping into solve?
And I, some of it sounds like those longer term questions, but.
Yeah.
I mean, right now it's a unified, like project management, people planning, like I'm making an org chart in Figma right now.
I also have like some stuff in Google Sheets and some stuff is in, no, again, still in Notion.
And, and so like, and the project tracking again is in linear.
So like, how do we.

Bring everything in one place is just like that's the biggest problem right now so that we can have like good strategic conversations that don't require a week of me like wrangling things across different places.
I don't have the time to wrangle everything in one place and think through the implications.
I'm kind of just I'm being too reactive right now.
And so I need somebody to help me like put in place the tooling.
Yeah.
Okay.
That is also a very familiar struggle.
Okay.
And then I think the last big question I have is I'm curious how.
I guess how you make operational changes.
I've worked at places where they were very OKR driven and I've worked at places where it was very like here's a problem.
Can you solve it by next week?
And places where, you know, you had to like run it past.
don't don't know

Five stakeholders with very tailored decks and get buy-in from each of them for even, you know, so I'm just curious, like, how changes happen and what that process kind of looks and feels like right now.
Right now, that's the key point to say is, like, right now.
I think it's, you see a problem, we're in a phase of memos, which, again, I don't know how long that'll last, but, like, this is a thing that needs to be changed.

This is a memo of how I'm thinking about changing it, like, and we have OKRs that different members of the leadership team are owners of, and then, you know, working with people on their teams to solve those aspects, like, here's a problem, what's your, how do we solve it?
Like, let's go after it.
It's, like, very, we're moving really fast.
If there's a solution, if there's a proposed solution, we try and get through.
Like, the decision process in, like, a week or two and, like, move fast on it if it's a problem that's a block or two, like, operations.
Okay.
And you differentiated right now as separate.
Do you have, like, a vision of how you want it to go?
Uh, I mean, I like the memo structure.
Like, um, I think Marcel modeled it on Amazon for better or worse, which is, like, just, like, get down your thinking, get by, and work through the structure.
Um, but, like, different team members have kind of a lot of leeway, and once something's approved, like, how they go about solving the problem on, like, the nitty-gritty basis.
Okay.
Okay, cool.
Well, those were my big three questions.
Is there anything else from your end I can help address?
No, this was a great conversation.
Great.
All right.
Well, thank you so much, then.
All right.
Thanks, Emily.
Have a good one.
You too.
Bye.

default_summary.markdown_formatted:
## Meeting Purpose

[Screening Emily Gutzmer for the Client Operations Manager role at GrowthX.](https://fathom.video/share/kwbYec39_E6RvAizV5y3cXThbWzfUEQk?tab=summary&timestamp=77.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [**The Problem:** GrowthX's fragmented ops (Linear, Notion, HubSpot) forces reactive work, blocking strategic planning for its pivot from agency to SaaS.](https://fathom.video/share/kwbYec39_E6RvAizV5y3cXThbWzfUEQk?tab=summary&timestamp=1594.0)
  - [**The Candidate:** Emily Gutzmer's experience at Airtable and Curated4U directly addresses this, having built foundational ops systems (ticketing, health scoring) from scratch.](https://fathom.video/share/kwbYec39_E6RvAizV5y3cXThbWzfUEQk?tab=summary&timestamp=345.0)
  - [**The Solution:** The role's immediate priority is consolidating tools into a single platform to enable proactive strategy and support the upcoming SaaS/PLG model.](https://fathom.video/share/kwbYec39_E6RvAizV5y3cXThbWzfUEQk?tab=summary&timestamp=1594.0)
  - [**The Fit:** Gutzmer's project examples show a strong grasp of both technical implementation (Zendesk) and the critical change management needed to drive adoption.](https://fathom.video/share/kwbYec39_E6RvAizV5y3cXThbWzfUEQk?tab=summary&timestamp=492.0)

## Topics

### The Problem: Fragmented Operations at GrowthX

  - [**Business Context:** GrowthX is pivoting from a marketing agency to a SaaS company with a services layer.](https://fathom.video/share/kwbYec39_E6RvAizV5y3cXThbWzfUEQk?tab=summary&timestamp=118.0)
      - [**Current State:** A 1-year-old agency using an internal AI platform for content creation.](https://fathom.video/share/kwbYec39_E6RvAizV5y3cXThbWzfUEQk?tab=summary&timestamp=105.0)
      - [**Future State:** The platform will become an external SaaS product with a PLG model.](https://fathom.video/share/kwbYec39_E6RvAizV5y3cXThbWzfUEQk?tab=summary&timestamp=120.0)
  - [**Operational Challenges:**](https://fathom.video/share/kwbYec39_E6RvAizV5y3cXThbWzfUEQk?tab=summary&timestamp=118.0)
      - [**Tooling:** Fragmented across Linear (strategic projects), Notion (health scoring), and HubSpot (limited seats).](https://fathom.video/share/kwbYec39_E6RvAizV5y3cXThbWzfUEQk?tab=summary&timestamp=224.0)
      - [**Impact:** This forces reactive work, preventing strategic analysis of team efficiency, customer health, and forecasting.](https://fathom.video/share/kwbYec39_E6RvAizV5y3cXThbWzfUEQk?tab=summary&timestamp=1639.0)
      - [**Goal:** Consolidate tools into a single platform for unified visibility and proactive planning.](https://fathom.video/share/kwbYec39_E6RvAizV5y3cXThbWzfUEQk?tab=summary&timestamp=1594.0)

### The Solution: Emily Gutzmer's Experience

  - [**Background:** Moved from Customer Success (Airtable) to Operations, driven by an interest in the consultative, operational aspects of the work.](https://fathom.video/share/kwbYec39_E6RvAizV5y3cXThbWzfUEQk?tab=summary&timestamp=345.0)
  - [**Project 1: Zendesk Launch (Curated4U)**](https://fathom.video/share/kwbYec39_E6RvAizV5y3cXThbWzfUEQk?tab=summary&timestamp=492.0)
      - [**Problem:** No formal client request process.](https://fathom.video/share/kwbYec39_E6RvAizV5y3cXThbWzfUEQk?tab=summary&timestamp=514.0)
      - [**Solution:** Launched Zendesk to create a structured ticketing system.](https://fathom.video/share/kwbYec39_E6RvAizV5y3cXThbWzfUEQk?tab=summary&timestamp=514.0)
      - [**Scope:**](https://fathom.video/share/kwbYec39_E6RvAizV5y3cXThbWzfUEQk?tab=summary&timestamp=540.0)
